preFix.title: Needle-felted hedgehog pin cushion
preFix.subtitle: A compact 8 cm hedgehog body in brown and cream merino, used as a sewing pin cushion
preFix.excerpt: Needle-felt a rounded hedgehog body in brown and cream merino, sculpt the nose and ears, and add stiff wool quills across the back. The finished hedgehog sits upright and holds pins in the quill area. A contained project that uses around 25 g of roving.
preFix.sourceNotes: Needle-felted hedgehog figures are a standard introductory sculpture project across UK and European felting workshops. Observation-led; the form and quill technique are consistent across guild teaching materials.

content.text: to build a compact rounded body from brown and cream

with short upright quills across the back that hold sewing pins securely. The hedgehog sits upright with a slightly pointed snout and small round ears. Finished size is approximately 8 cm from nose to tail and 5 cm tall. The body is worked from a rough oval base shape that is built up gradually; no armature is needed.

attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Never lever sideways. Keep your supporting hand clear of the needle path at all times.
attrs.title: Needle safety

items.name: Brown merino roving for the body, 20 g
Cream or buff merino roving for the belly and snout, 5 g
Dark brown or black roving for the nose, tiny pinch
Felting foam mat, at least 15 x 15 cm

content.text: Take a 15 g length of brown roving and fold it back on itself three times to form a rough oval approximately 10 x 7 cm. Begin needling through the surface with the 38-gauge needle, working in straight vertical stabs 1 to 2 cm deep across the whole surface. Rotate the oval and needle the underside and sides. Continue for 10 to 15 minutes, rotating frequently, until the oval begins to compact and hold its shape without unravelling. Do not needle too deeply at the base: the bottom surface should remain slightly flat so the hedgehog sits steadily.

Pinch one end of the oval slightly and needle into it at an angle to draw it forward into a blunt point. Add a small wisp of cream roving over the snout area and needle it in for a lighter face. Add a tiny pinch of dark roving at the tip and needle it into a firm nose dot approximately 5 mm across. Work slowly with the fine 40-gauge needle for detail: the smaller needle gives more precise placement on the nose.

Take a wisp of cream roving and lay it across the underside of the body, covering approximately two thirds of the base from the snout toward the rear. Needle it in evenly until it blends smoothly into the brown sides. The belly patch gives the hedgehog its characteristic pale underside and the seam where brown and cream meet is softened by needling across the boundary from both sides.
Step 4: Make and attach the ears

Pull two small pinches of brown roving and roll each into a rough oval. Needle each one on the foam mat until it is a small flat oval approximately 1 cm long and firm enough to hold its shape. Place each ear flat against the top of the head just behind the snout and needle along the lower edge to bond the ear to the head. The ears should stand slightly upright.

Quills are made by felting short lengths of roving into stiff upright spines. Pull wisps of brown roving approximately 4 cm long and 5 mm wide. Fold each wisp in half, place the folded end against the back of the hedgehog, and needle through the folded end only, leaving the free ends extending upright. Work from the rear of the back toward the shoulders, placing quills 5 to 8 mm apart. Ten to fifteen quills across the back is enough to hold pins securely. The free ends should be stiff enough to retain their shape; if they droop, needle the base more firmly.

items.fix: Add wisps of roving to hollows and needle them in, then work across the whole body surface to even the density. A smooth shape comes from gradual building, not from heavy needling in one area.
items.cause: The roving was folded unevenly and the needling did not compress all areas equally
items.problem: The body is lumpy and uneven in shape

items.fix: Needle the folded base of each quill more thoroughly. The free ends stand upright only when the base is well locked into the body surface.
items.cause: The base of each quill was not needled firmly enough into the body
items.problem: The quills do not stand upright and collapse against the back

items.fix: Always withdraw the needle in the same line as entry. Keep spare needles; breakage is a material reality with very dense felt and is not a technique error.
items.cause: The needle was levered sideways rather than withdrawn straight
